quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Linear momentum"?

options.text: F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Linear momentum" is defined as: p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Impulse"?

options.text: J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
An orbit with T = 24 hours at r ≈ 42,200 km from Earth's center; the satellite stays above one point on the equator
r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Impulse" is defined as: J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Impulse-momentum theorem"?

options.text: W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial

quiz_questions.explanation: "Impulse-momentum theorem" is defined as: The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's Second Law (momentum form)"?

options.text: F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals the time rate of change of momentum
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's Second Law (momentum form)" is defined as: F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals the time rate of change of momentum. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Impulse approximation"?

options.text: a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes
A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k)
In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: "Impulse approximation" is defined as: In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s"?

options.text: Energy dissipation
Linear momentum
Parallel axis theorem
Internal vs. external forces

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Linear momentum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s"?

options.text: Rotational kinematics
Conservation of angular momentum
Atwood machine
Impulse

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Impulse".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial"?

options.text: Work-Energy Theorem
Impulse-momentum theorem
Superposition
Angular frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Impulse-momentum theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals the time rate of change of momentum"?

options.text: Tension
Efficiency
Newton's Second Law (momentum form)
Angular frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's Second Law (momentum form)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision"?

options.text: Kinetic friction
Conservation of mechanical energy
Impulse approximation
Work by a spring

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Impulse approximation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Momentum is accurate?

options.text: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.
Conservation of Energy states that in an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant when only conservative forces do work: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Momentum?

options.text: The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Momentum?

options.text: Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
The impulse-momentum theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp) connects the time integral of force to the change in momentum, making it especially useful for analyzing collisions and impacts.
Vector addition, subtraction, dot product (A·B = ABcosθ), and cross product (A×B, magnitude = ABsinθ) are essential operations throughout mechanics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Momentum?

options.text: Systematic problem-solving involves drawing free-body diagrams, choosing a coordinate system, applying ΣF = ma in each direction, and solving the resulting system of equations.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
This principle is derived directly from Newton's Third Law—internal forces between objects cancel in pairs.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Momentum?

options.text: Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Momentum?

options.text: Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Momentum?

options.text: Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Sports equipment design (golf clubs, tennis rackets) optimizing mass ratios and coefficients of restitution for maximum energy transfer to the ball

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Momentum applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force
Firearms forensics using conservation of momentum to calculate bullet speed from the recoil velocity of a ballistic pendulum
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Momentum?

options.text: Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Momentum in practice?

options.text: Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum.
